abstract: This study examines the demographic, institutional, and economic characteristics of the individuals responsible for over 750 of science’s most significant breakthroughs, encompassing both Nobel Prize winners and major non-Nobel discoverers. By analyzing a comprehensive dataset of 982 scientists across history, the research identifies a major shift in the profile of the scientific elite toward greater interdisciplinarity, institutional elitism, and more advanced age. Findings reveal that 54% of Nobel-winning discoveries were made by researchers with degrees in at least two different fields, suggesting that interdisciplinary training provides the methodological versatility essential for innovation. The results further indicate that science is increasingly concentrated in elite environments, with 30% of major discoveries occurring at the world’s top 25 universities. Additionally, the age at which scientists make significant contributions has risen: while historically common at younger ages, major discoveries by scientists over 60 have become exceptionally rare, with the current "golden age" for productivity falling between 35 and 45. The study also highlights persistent disparities, noting the continued underrepresentation of women and a decline in the dominance of researchers based in Europe in favor of North America. These trends suggest that while the "burden of knowledge" requires longer periods of training and specialized environments, scientific progress could be further accelerated if funding agencies and academic institutions countered narrow specialization by incentivizing interdisciplinary research and broader methodological integration.
